title: Rendering React - Mounting to DOM
description: Learn how to render React components to the DOM and understand the rendering process.
longDescription: You've created components and mastered JSX - now it's time to bring them to life! In this lesson, you'll learn how React renders components to the DOM, understand the role of ReactDOM, and create your first complete React application that displays on a web page.

learningObjectives.statement: Explain how React renders components to the DOM
Use ReactDOM.createRoot to mount a React app
Structure a basic React application with App component

missionNarrative.title: The Great Rendering
missionNarrative.story: Your components are ready, but they're trapped in code! To bring them to life, you must perform the ancient ritual of Rendering. With ReactDOM as your guide, you'll mount your components to the DOM and watch them spring into existence!

sections.title: What is Rendering?
sections.content:
**Rendering** is the process of displaying your React components on a web page.

React components are just JavaScript functions that return JSX. To actually see them in a browser, you need to **render** them to the **DOM** (Document Object Model).

**The rendering process:**
1. React creates a **virtual representation** of your UI (Virtual DOM)
2. ReactDOM takes this virtual DOM and updates the **real browser DOM**
3. Your components appear on the screen!

This separation allows React to be incredibly efficient!

sections.title: Basic Rendering Example
The Root Element
sections.content:
Most React apps have a single root element in their HTML: ```html
``` This empty `
` is where React takes over. Everything inside will be managed by React!

sections.title: The App Component Pattern
Complete React Application
What is StrictMode?
sections.content:
`<React.StrictMode>` is a development tool that helps catch potential problems:

```javascript
root.render(
  <React.StrictMode>
    <App />
  </React.StrictMode>
);
```

**StrictMode does NOT render anything visible.** It:
- Checks for deprecated patterns
- Warns about unsafe lifecycles
- Detects side effects
- Only runs in development (not production)

Keep it enabled while learning!

sections.title: Understanding the Virtual DOM
Rendering Multiple Components
Important: Call render Once

challenges.title: Rendering Order
challenges.description: Put the rendering steps in the correct order
challenges.question: What is the correct order for rendering a React app?
challenges.instructions: Select the correct sequence of steps.

options.text: 1. Create component → 2. Get root element → 3. Create root → 4. Call render()
options.explanation: Correct! This is the proper rendering sequence.

options.text: 1. Call render() → 2. Create component → 3. Get root element
options.explanation: Wrong! You can't render before creating the root.

options.text: 1. Get root element → 2. Create root → 3. Create component → 4. Call render()
options.explanation: Also correct! You can get the root element before or after creating components.

options.text: 1. Create root → 2. Call render() → 3. Create component
options.explanation: Nope! You need a component before you can render it.

challenges.successFeedback: Great! You understand the rendering flow!
challenges.failureFeedback: Remember: create components first, then get the root, create root, and finally render.

hints.text: You need something to render before you can call render().
ReactDOM.createRoot needs a DOM element to work with.
The render() method is always the last step.

hints.finalExplanation: The correct flow is: 1) Create your components, 2) Get the root DOM element, 3) Create a React root with createRoot(), 4) Call render() with your component.

challenges.title: Virtual DOM Concepts
challenges.description: Test your understanding of the Virtual DOM
challenges.question: Which statements about the Virtual DOM are true?

options.text: The Virtual DOM is a lightweight copy of the real DOM
options.explanation: Correct! React keeps a virtual copy in memory.

options.text: React updates the entire real DOM on every change
options.explanation: Nope! React only updates what changed - that's the point!

options.text: React compares Virtual DOM versions to find differences
The Virtual DOM makes React faster than manual DOM manipulation
options.explanation: Correct! Selective updates are much more efficient.

options.text: You directly manipulate the Virtual DOM in your code
options.explanation: No! React manages the Virtual DOM. You just update state.

challenges.successFeedback: Excellent! You understand how the Virtual DOM works!
challenges.failureFeedback: Remember: Virtual DOM is React's optimization technique for efficient updates.

hints.text: The Virtual DOM is React's internal optimization mechanism.
React compares versions to minimize real DOM updates.

challenges.title: Fix the Rendering Code
challenges.description: Debug and fix broken rendering code
challenges.question: Fix the errors in this rendering code
challenges.instructions: Fill in the blanks to make the code render correctly.
challenges.successFeedback: Perfect! The rendering code is fixed!
challenges.failureFeedback: Check the ReactDOM import path and method names carefully.

hints.text: In React 18+, ReactDOM is imported from 'react-dom/client'.
Use getElementById to get the DOM element.
Create a root first, then call render on that root.

challenges.title: Build a Complete App
challenges.description: Create a complete React app with multiple components
challenges.question: Create and render a complete React application
challenges.instructions: Create three components (Header, Main, Footer) and an App component that combines them. Then render the App to the DOM.
challenges.successFeedback: Amazing! You've built and rendered a complete React application!
challenges.failureFeedback: Make sure you have all three components, combine them in App, and render App to the DOM.

hints.text: Create three separate function components: Header, Main, and Footer.
Each component should return JSX with appropriate HTML elements.
The App component should include all three components as children.

reflectionQuestion.question: Why do you think React uses a Virtual DOM instead of directly updating the real DOM? Describe a scenario where this would make a big difference in performance.
reflectionQuestion.sampleAnswer: The Virtual DOM allows React to batch changes and only update what's necessary. For example, if you have a list of 1000 items and only one changes, React only updates that one item instead of re-rendering the entire list. This makes apps much faster, especially with frequent updates.
